title: Cat Sneezing and Green Nasal Discharge: Causes and Triage Guide
meta_desc: Is your cat sneezing with green discharge? Learn what it means, the urgency level for vet care, and why photos help with veterinary triage.
content_html:
What is Green Nasal Discharge in Cats?

When a cat has green or yellow nasal discharge, it is often a sign of a secondary bacterial infection. While clear discharge can be related to simple allergies or a mild viral flare-up (like Feline Herpesvirus), thick and colored mucus indicates that the immune system is actively fighting an infection in the upper respiratory tract or sinuses.

Is it an Emergency?

The urgency level for this condition is Medium. It is generally not a life-threatening emergency unless your cat is having significant difficulty breathing or has stopped eating entirely. However, because bacterial infections in the respiratory tract rarely resolve on their own, you should schedule a veterinary appointment within 24 to 48 hours to prevent the condition from progressing to pneumonia.

Why a Photo Helps Triage

Providing your veterinarian with a clear, close-up photo of the nasal discharge can be extremely beneficial. Since cats often groom themselves and wipe away mucus before they arrive at the clinic, a photo ensures the vet sees the true color, consistency, and volume of the discharge. This helps them determine the severity of the infection and whether immediate diagnostic imaging or specific antibiotics are required.

faqs.question: Can I use human cold medicine for my cat?
faqs.answer: No, never give your cat human medications. Many over-the-counter decongestants and pain relievers are highly toxic to felines.

faqs.question: What are common causes of colored discharge?
faqs.answer: Common causes include feline respiratory disease complex (cat flu), nasal polyps, foreign bodies stuck in the nose, or advanced dental disease.

faqs.question: Is green nasal discharge contagious to other pets?
faqs.answer: Yes, many upper respiratory infections are highly contagious between cats. It is best to isolate the symptomatic cat until they have been evaluated by a vet.
